one_line_takeaway: AnaFP replaces the guesswork in adversarial-example DNN fingerprinting with a derived admissible interval for how far to push a fingerprint from the decision boundary, jointly guaranteeing robustness and uniqueness and topping heuristic baselines across CNN/MLP/GNN models — though knowledge-distillation attacks still degrade it.
key_finding: Choosing the boundary distance analytically (feasible tau inside the admissible interval) beats heuristic distance selection. AnaFP attains the highest AUC on all four model/dataset settings (e.g., 0.957 on CNN/CIFAR-10 vs next-best UAP 0.850) and leads under every non-trivial modification attack. Ablations confirm the mechanism: fixing tau to a single bound (C-lower/C-upper) or a uniform margin (C-fix) underperforms the interval search, and the quantile threshold governs a clear feasibility/quality tradeoff (too strict yields zero valid fingerprints; too loose adds low-quality ones).
core_contribution: AnaFP, an analytical adversarial-example-based DNN fingerprinting scheme for model-ownership/IP verification. Its central novelty is turning the previously heuristic choice of fingerprint-to-boundary distance into a theoretically grounded one: the authors introduce a scalar 'stretch factor' tau that scales a minimal decision-altering perturbation, and derive a closed-form admissible interval (tau_lower, tau_upper) in which tau must lie to simultaneously satisfy a robustness constraint (fingerprint survives performance-preserving model modifications) and a uniqueness constraint (fingerprint is not matched by independently trained models). tau_lower ties to robustness via the logit shift under modification and the logit-margin gradient norm c_g at the boundary point; tau_upper ties to uniqueness via a minimum independent-model margin m_min and a local Lipschitz bound L_uniq. Prior work (IPGuard, MarginFinger, etc.) is cited as picking this distance empirically; that is background, not AnaFP's contribution.
threat_model: Two parties: a model owner who trains and deploys a protected DNN P as a (black-box) service, and an attacker who obtains an unauthorized copy of P, applies performance-preserving model-modification attacks (fine-tuning, pruning, knowledge distillation, adversarial training, and composites like N-finetune, P-finetune, Prune-KD) to shift the decision boundary and evade detection, then redistributes the pirated model as a black-box API. The owner verifies ownership by black-box querying a suspect model S with a fingerprint set F and thresholding the matching rate. Goal: flag pirated models (set V_P) while not falsely attributing independently trained models (set I_P). Verification is query-only/black-box; no access to suspect weights is assumed.
limitations: 1) Knowledge-distillation-style attacks remain the weak spot: AUC drops to 0.756 (KD) and 0.689 (Prune-KD) — still best-in-class but far from reliable, since KD discards internal weights/structure that the boundary-based fingerprints rely on. 2) The theoretical guarantee is weakened in practice by two approximations the authors acknowledge: replacing infinite model sets with finite surrogate pools (approximation error is 'theoretically intractable' and surrogate-dependent) and quantile relaxation of the bounds. 3) Verification performance depends on quantile threshold choice (Table 3): strict settings 0.1/0.9 and 0.2/0.8 produce zero valid fingerprints. 4) Some baselines (UAP, MarginFinger, ADV-TRA, GMFIP) are inapplicable to GNNs, limiting head-to-head comparison there. 5) Scope is classification DNNs (CNN/MLP/GNN); no evaluation on large generative or foundation models.

quantitative_results: AUC (main, Table 1): AnaFP 0.957 (CNN/CIFAR-10), 0.893 (CNN/CIFAR-100), 0.963 (MLP/MNIST), 0.926 (GNN/PROTEINS) — highest in every column
Next-best baseline AUCs: UAP 0.850 (CIFAR-10), 0.806 (CIFAR-100), 0.906 (MNIST); AKH 0.854 (PROTEINS); IPGuard 0.715 (CIFAR-10)
AUC under attacks, CNN (Table 2): Pruning 1.000, Fine-tuning 0.979, KD 0.756, AT 0.983, N-finetune 0.978, P-finetune 0.989, Prune-KD 0.689
Under pruning all methods near-perfect (AUC >= 0.999); AnaFP leads all other attacks

Quantile-threshold sweep (Table 3, q_margin/q_lip): 0.1/0.9 and 0.2/0.8 -> 0 valid fingerprints (no AUC); 0.3/0.7 -> 11 fingerprints, AUC 0.848; 0.5/0.5 -> 56 fp, AUC 0.957; 0.6/0.4 -> 103 fp, AUC 0.959 (peak); 0.9/0.1 -> 1158 fp, AUC 0.896
Surrogate pool: AUC stabilizes once pool size > 6 models; low-diversity setting still yields AUC 0.912
Testing benchmark: 120 pirated + 120 independent models per task; all runs repeated 5 times (mean +/- std reported)
baselines_compared: UAP (Peng et al. 2022, universal adversarial perturbations)
GMFIP (Yan et al. 2025, generator-synthesized non-adversarial fingerprints)
ADV-TRA (Xu et al. 2024, adversarial trajectories across multiple boundaries)
